core_mechanism: Hospital pressure is visible and urgent; upstream access failure is dispersed and delayed.
Separate appropriations reduce direct diversion but not strategic salience and transfer-friction problems.
Fixed per-person funding weakens marginal supply for additional eligible care.
Patients choose between waiting, paying, delaying, telehealth, ambulance, urgent care and ED.
Locality/population functions may add value; payment intermediation/pass-through may add opacity.
ACC activity payments and contracts may sustain supply but can also create arbitrage.
Ambulance can be an upstream treatment/referral service, not only ED conveyance.
Funding rules can bottleneck supply by profession rather than clinical scope.
Digital access can extend care or hollow local in-person capacity.
Co-payments can signal demand or create inequitable unmet need.
What is top-tier reported becomes managed.
Transactional access does not replace culturally safe relational/community-led care.
Reform can be framed as pro-market, anti-PHO, anti-GP, anti-equity or pro-patient.
Unmet need remains less fundable if it is invisible.
Uncapped benefits need population obligations or providers may select easy work.
Separate buckets protect funding but may also impede whole-system resource shifts.
Face-to-face urgent care can deflect ED demand or absorb scarce resources.
Tactical initiatives need one integrated architecture.
Removing global caps improves marginal supply but requires item-level controls, audit and equity safeguards.
